analysis:
Across the stronger heuristics (notably 1–5, 7–9, 12–14) a clear pattern emerges: (1) deterministic Pareto prefilter to reduce N, (2) cheap per-point proxies (axis-aligned box volume ± few-weight scalarizations) to rank and form a k‑proportional shortlist, (3) a CELF/lazy-greedy or greedy insertion using cached singleton HVs (selective pairwise caching when shortlist is small), and (4) bounded deterministic 1-for-1 local swaps. These designs minimize expensive hypervolume (HV) calls by (a) precomputing singletons, (b) lazy re-evaluation with timestamps, and (c) cheap upper‑bound pruning (box-volume or scalar proxies). They also use deterministic seeds and stable tie‑breakers so outputs are reproducible.

Weaker examples (e.g., 6, parts of 10 and 15) tend to be expensive or fragile: many unguarded full HV recomputations, lack of singleton/pair caches, repeated reallocations/copies, membership tests with O(N) list "in" checks, random non-deterministic initializations, or direct exhaustive swap loops without pruning or HV budgets. Avoidable overheads seen across variants include recomputing identical HVs, rebuilding arrays repeatedly instead of indexing, excessive weight/vector counts without trimming, and not bounding HV calls (leading to worst-case heavy runtimes). Good tradeoffs in the top heuristics: keep shortlist size adaptive to k/pareto size, cache singletons (and small pairwise tables), use CELF lazy timestamps to amortize HV work, use cheap proxies for pruning, and limit swap iterations or HV-budget to control runtime.
experience: Enforce a strict HV‑call budget and use CELF lazy greedy with cached singleton (and selective pairwise) HVs. Build an adaptive, k‑proportional shortlist via a blended box‑volume + few‑weight scalar score, prune with cheap upper bounds, and use O(1) set membership and index-based array views to avoid copying. Add deterministic tie‑breaking and a tiny bounded 1‑for‑1 swap cleanup. Consider parallel HV evaluations or an incremental HV routine if available.
current_reflection: Enforce a strict HV‑call budget and use CELF lazy greedy with cached singleton (and selective pairwise) HVs. Build an adaptive, k‑proportional shortlist via a blended box‑volume + few‑weight scalar score, prune with cheap upper bounds, and use O(1) set membership and index-based array views to avoid copying. Add deterministic tie‑breaking and a tiny bounded 1‑for‑1 swap cleanup. Consider parallel HV evaluations or an incremental HV routine if available.
good_reflections: Prefilter dominated points, approximate HVC with few weight vectors to shortlist candidates, then perform a local search using cached incremental HVC deltas. Minimize Python-side HV calls by batching and avoid nested HV recomputations. Make the algorithm deterministic (seeded) and ensure it always returns exactly k points.
bad_reflections: Make next‑gen heuristics: (1) rank by cheap proxies to form an adaptive shortlist, (2) cache singleton HVs and use CELF lazy updates, (3) prune with box‑volume/singleton upper bounds and cap HV calls, (4) avoid Python O(N) membership in inner loops (use sets/indices), and (5) only pairwise‑cache when shortlist ≲40. Keep shortlist size adaptive to k and Pareto size.

Use an adaptive shortlist (k- and Pareto-aware), compute cheap HVC approximations (many weight vectors) to rank candidates, run CELF with cached singleton HVs + timestamps, prune with tight box-volume/upper bounds, enforce an HV-call budget, and do a capped candidate-limited deterministic swap pass using incremental HV deltas. This yields reproducible, high-quality subsets with predictable runtime.

Use CELF with cached singletons + selective pairwise HVs, build an adaptive shortlist via moderate-weight scalar HVC and box‑upper bounds, enforce an HV‑call budget, vectorize and reuse buffers, limit targeted swaps, and prefer deterministic seeds. Consider incremental HV updates or parallel HV evaluations to boost exact checks.
comprehensive_reflection:
- Keywords: CELF; cached singleton HVC; adaptive shortlist; box‑volume upper bound; HV‑call budget; deterministic ties; incremental/parallel HV; bounded swap.
- Advice: Enforce an HV‑call budget; form a k‑proportional shortlist scored by blended box‑volume + few‑weight scalar proxies; run CELF lazy greedy with cached singletons and selective pairwise caches; prune via cheap upper bounds; use index views and O(1) membership; deterministically tie‑break; finish with a tiny bounded 1‑for‑1 swap.
- Avoid: Per‑candidate Python HV calls, nested HV recomputation, nondeterministic selection, O(N) inner‑loop membership checks, unbounded pairwise caching or oversized shortlists.
- Explanation: Minimizes costly HV evaluations, gives predictable runtime, reproducible results and high‑quality k‑subsets with minimal cleanup.
